sentences.text: Probably by connection, one or the other.
And he lived there.
And certain days, the Germans used to bring up Jews.
And I used to look out through small spaces and see how they killed Jews.
They used to bring them there, certain times, five, six, 10 Jews, whoever they found hiding.
To that castle-- there was acreage of land and--
They used to shoot.
And we used to see.
Well, after a while, we were notified that the, the princess, whoever she was, that she started getting scared.
It was too hard for her to cook for so many people.
And it started to be a little bit suspicious, because certain people ask her why certain rooms are blocked off with bricks or-- and she started to be scared.
So was he.
And he suggested that we move to the camp, the huge camp, which was a very-- clear of use.
There was nobody there-- to, to get, to get out of the castle and live there on the attic.
And-- How long were you in the castle, do you think?
Any--
It had to be like-- maybe July till-- July till, till late fall.
Or maybe-- yeah, till late fall.
And he told us that we have to-- at night, small groups of two, or one, or three-- go through from the castle to the camp, which was-- the camp was in center city.
It was-- used to be, years ago, a military camp, a Polish military camp.
It was in the center of the city.
And, and we had to go through side streets, through, through certain block and come there.
He will stay with his SS uniform and let us in.
Well, I, I went myself.
I was a little boy.
And I knew all my side streets.
And I came.
And, and it was just in the evening when German soldiers came out from, from the movie.
And I-- now, I'm not sure which German I should approach.
So I waited.
It was near a park.
And I waited.
And I walked around and hide.
And then I saw that  they disappeared.
And he still-- this German SS man is standing in front of the gate.
And I went over.
And he grabbed me and pushed me in the camp.
And we went up on the attic.
And--
No.
Never saw him.
Never saw him.
But I knew he was going to be dressed in German uniform.
And after a few hours, we all concentrated in that attic.
And he tried to bring us bread.
And what we saw in the attic is we saw a lot of American money torn up in pieces.
I found American money torn in pieces-- dollars or, or any other currency.
And I was asking, was is that?
He said, well, people used to probably hide it.
And before they went to death, they just tore it apart so the Germans don't get a hold of it.
Probably a lot of Jewish people had money.
And I found a lot of small pieces of all kind of currency.
This was on the attic.
We were there-- I don't think we were there more than a week or two.
And he came and notified us that he had some connections in villages.
Apparently, he didn't want to kill us, maybe for protection again, maybe-- I couldn't tell why.
But he told us that we're going to move at night in groups to, to certain farmers.
We're going to live in farms.
My sister and, like, three or four other people went to one farmer.
I was sent to another farmer.
He lived in [INAUDIBLE] by himself.
His name was Kotofski.
He kept 23 Jews on top of a barn.
And he had, I think, three or four daughters.
And his wife-- his wife was Ukrainian.
And he lived there.
And he, he was very nice to us.
He used to bring food.
Again, we paid him as much, whoever had money, so he can buy the food.
But do you imagine to feed 23, 24 people?
A lot of cooking, and a lot of bread, and it was very suspicious to go and buy bread.
Because they would know right away [? for his family. ?] What I remember is that one time, they said that his wife tried to run away, because it was so hard for her.
And she just want to go-- run away from him and maybe tell the police about us.
And he caught her on the way.
And, and he beat her up or something like this.
Now, we were there till winter.
I remember it was a very hard winter.
And, and you have to understand, in this part of Poland, which is Galicia, there was a lot of Ukrainian.
And there was a lot of Polish people.
Ukrainian hated Polish people, because the Ukrainian-- probably, some of them were a little segregated under the Polish regime.
But the, the, the Ukrainian cooperated with Germans.
And, and the Germans had a SS division, a Ukrainian SS division, which collaborated with the Germans of killing Jews, mostly watching the camps.
Now, the Ukrainian want to take revenge on the Polish people from the previous war.
It started with, with killing, with burning villages.
We were smack in the middle.
We were with this man.
The funny part-- that his sister was married to SS man in Germany.
And she knew about us.
And her husband didn't know.
And she approved it [INAUDIBLE].
The rumor was that he said if he lived through the war, he would like to be written in in the Jewish history.
Fine.
So-- and on Christmas night in 19-- in 1943, we heard a terrible noise.
And it was like sleighs, three, four sleighs with people, with horses.
And it was a lot of shooting.
And they start throwing fire, flames, you know, torches.
And as we look down, we saw that they killed the whole family.
They were in the house, celebrating Christmas Eve.
We were on the attic in the, in the barn where he kept his cows.
And he had a very, very famous bull, I remember.
And this was his business, that people from the neighborhood used to come with the cows.
So-- well, then they start throwing torches on top of the barn.
And the barn will start burn, because the roofs were built from straw.
So we decided to jump.
We had to jump and save.
And they, they were on the way to go already.
But when they saw people jumping, I was told later that they thought that maybe some Polish partisans or Russian partisans came, you know?
And they ran away.
They left us.
So we spread out in small groups to the woods.
Me and my sister was together with another girl who lives now in Australia.
And she was with us and another few people.
And we ran into the woods.
We organized ourselves.
Certain people went one direction.
You know, it was very disorganized.
It was-- you know.
Everybody thought they was gonna die.
And we wandered the whole night through the snow until we came to a little house, to a little Polish house, again, in [INAUDIBLE], which w-- which was abandoned.
And we slept over.
We used to go in the-- the farmers who lived, they were self-sufficient people.
So if they had potatoes or cabbage or-- they used to save it underground.
They used to dig holes, cover it with straw so it wouldn't get frozen, and then summertime, use it.
We slept over.
And we went into one of those underground-- I can't remember if it was cabbage or potatoes.
We slept over.
The next day, we tried to dry out, start a fire on the fireplace, you know?
And we saw, we saw white-- people with white uniforms.
We knew they got to be Ukrainian.
So we start running, again, into the woods.
I lost my shoes.
And I, I run barefooted.
We run into the woods.
And we run.
I froze up my feet.
And then I was, you know, saved by pe-- our people by rubbing the feet--
With snow.
And once I, I felt the temperature back, they took rags.
And we wandered around till late day.
And we saw a house in the morning.
And there was a Polish lady, again, by itself in [INAUDIBLE], with a man.
And they took us in.
They fed us.
They gave us a little food.
And they kept us for a few days.
Must be six or eight people.
And somehow, the people who were with us, one of the men, who was a very-- who was-- this was the man who my sister was engaged to him.
He took care of us from the day that our sister was taken and my mother.
He's the guy who used to sell for us, give us advice.
I mean, we lived with our aunt and uncle.
But he gave us advice.
And he was with us.
Somehow, he had some connection.
And, and they say they're going to come with sleighs through the woods.
They're going to take us to a Polish village.
And the name was Huta Pieniacka.
And they're going to spread us out, like two, three in each family.
Whoever has money, you're gonna pay them.
So I was sent-- I was sent with that, that girl that's today in Australia, Sheila, and somebody else, I remember-- and with the first sled with the horses.
It was very late afternoon.
And we came to that village.
And we were put in a Polish family.
And my sister's supposed to arrive with another sled and another group.
And there was a third group, probably.
Well, we waited.
We waited.
And supposed to only be 20 minutes or a half an hour later.
But nobody shows up.
Late, late in the evening, my sister showed up by herself in the village.
She knew the name.
She has [INAUDIBLE].
And she told us that-- us that what they did is they probably took people who they know that we don't nothing, like me and that girl.
They send them first.
Apparently, my sister was in another sleigh with that, with that man who was engaged to my sister.
And he always wore a gold watch, I remember.
It was very-- they want to rob him.
So what they did is they robbed him and they killed him.
But my sister-- either intentionally, they let her survive, run away, or she did it.
But she run away by foot.
And she told me that other people ran away.
They let them run away, or they run away.
So she joined us.
And we were in that house.
We lived in that Polish village.
Apparently, one day, they told us that the SS, the Ukrainian cell division-- is going to come to examine the village.
There were two rumors.
One rumor was that they found out that Jewish people are-- they're saving Jewish people.
Huta Pieniascka.
And, and the other rumor was that a few weeks ago, you see, the Polish people who lived in the village, they had a few rifles to protect themself.
Because there was going on a war between Ukrainian and Poland.
The other rumor was that two SS policemen wanted to come in.
And they killed the Polish.
So they want to come in.
And then they gave an order to the Polish people to surrender the, the rifle.
They're going to come in with German police.
And they're going to inspect the village.
But let, let me just go back a little bit.
When we lived in the village, one day, Russian partisans arrived.
And [? we thought we are-- ?] that's it.
That's the end.
Apparently, the Russian used to send Russian partisans to, to damage and to derail.
And when the partisans came, they would take with them men 18 and over-- they would take with them to the partisans.
But again, women and children, they didn't.
So after three days, we had [? a thrill. ?] And then they left.
And again, we left in the village.
And then started a story with the Ukrainian-- that night, we didn't sleep.
Everybody was dressed, ready.
And around-- what happened is they came, the Germans.
And they surrounded the village, because it was heavy wooded all around.
By surrounding the village, around 2:00 o'clock in the morning, 2:30-- and suddenly, they opened up fire from all the directions.
Me and my sister and the gal-- there was another man-- we ran straight through the snow to the woods.
But they were all around us, shooting from every direction.
I saw a lot of Polish people falling in front of us, getting killed.
I fell on top of dead people.
Just continued till we hit the woods.
When we came into the woods, I lost my sister.
I didn't know where she is.
So I ran-- I was alone.
I ran.
And, and then maybe two hours later, I saw some people.
And I knew they're not Germans, because they were deep in the woods and were huge people.
And those huge people took me with them.
And they were also wandering from other villages.
And they had a group.
You see, there were three Polish villages.
They called them Huta Pieniacka, Huta [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE].
And one was from the other maybe three, four kilometer.
So we wandered among those villages.
And they had a place where they used to sleep at night by a fire.
And I lost my sister.
And I didn't know if she got killed or she is alive.
And I was with that group.
One night, came a few Jews form another group, you know?
There was a problem to be in the woods.
You have to know now, because there were a lot of disappointed Ukrainian, which were [? bandera, ?] they called them.
There was a leader who was promised by the Germans to get a free Ukrainian.
And then they turned against-- well, they turned against Polish and then against Russia.
So they're trained in the woods.
The Polish survivors, they are trained in the woods.
And then the Jews was-- who wanted to survive, they lived in the woods.
So now we have to watch the Ukrainian.
We have to watch the Polish.
So told, they told us to be, OK.
On top of everything, the German used to surround the woods with cannons.
And what they did is just shoot the cannons to the woods.
And the noise, you know, among trees is very loud, very terrible.
Those days were the most horrible days, because we used to run maybe 15, 20 miles a day, nowhere.
You ran around.
And we came back to the same place.
I had shoes.
I had wooden shoes, I remember I had wooden shoes.
Maybe four or five numbers bigger that somebody gave me in the village.
You lived in, in the woods.
It was nothing.
It was, it was a fire.
There was straw.
And we ate-- we used to go back at night to the vil-- to the Polish villages and the pigs and cows that the Germans killed.
It was winter.
Had frozen meat.
It was plentiful.
You, you, you had plenty.
You didn't have bread.
You didn't have salt.
But meat, you had.
And then we used to go on in those underground bunkers.
You had potatoes.
You had cabbage, you know?
So we had what to eat.
Every night, we used to go get some stuff.
Get food and bring it back.
And one day came a group.
And we were talking-- there's a group hiding in this direction.
There's a group hiding in this direction.
And they told me that two Jewish girl from our village survived.
But there in the [INAUDIBLE] between, between the other village and the third village, there's a bunker in the woods.
So I figured, it's got to be my sister maybe.
They give me the directions.
So he told me, get into the other village.
You, you see, you have to understand.
In those condition, your instinct is very-- it's like-- sometime I see those people in jungle.
I mean, their instinct is amazing.
And I went there and decided I'm gonna go look for my sister.
And I went.
And I stopped afternoon to the second village, which was still existing, the Polish village.
And the first house, I ask, how-- give me the directions to go to [?
Maidan, ?] which is the third village.
And they told me, just follow to the third village with a dirt road in the woods.
You'll see the-- you'll find them.
You-- how do you find it?
He says, you will see manure.
People, they go out.
And they make on the snow.
And you're going to see it.
So I went into the village.
And I asked the first man, how do I go to [?
Maidan? ?] Which directions in the wood?
And he kicked me.
And he said, on account of you Jews, they'll burn this village, too.
It's all your fault.
On account of you, they burned [INAUDIBLE], because you were Jewish.
And they knew that they're hiding Jews.
So I don't want to even see you.
And he kicked me.
So I decided to go.
However, he told me, go this way.
And he kicked me.
And I went.
And night fell.
And, and it's-- you walk by yourself at night in the woods, it's, it's scary.
And after walking maybe, I don't know, maybe an hour, an hour and a half, I saw manure.
So I followed.
And I came to a bunker.
They were underground.
And I stuck my head.
And in Yiddish, I start screaming.
You know, is somebody here?
So a guy came out.
And I says, do you, do you know about two Jewish girls who came from a village?
He says, yes, they're here.
And here ran up-- my sister comes out with that girl.
I says, join me.
Let's go back where I am among Jews.
Because they were underground.
It was a lot of water there.
Our conditions were much better, like other different people.
So we, we travelled back.
And we stayed with that group.
And again, every night, we go to the villages.
You'll get.
And this happened late-- I think the beginning of March in 1944.
We went to the village.
We slept overnight, you know, to take potatoes.
And we got ready all the things to take back.
And as we open the hatch, we saw horses going back and forth, back and forth.
And as we looked, we didn't know that those are Ukraines, or those are Russians, or those-- we didn't know.
You know, that time, it was a mess.
But then we looked, somebody said that he saw a red-- like a Russian soldier with a, with a star.
We open up.
And we start running [INAUDIBLE] us.
In the beginning, they questioned us-- who we are.
And we told them that we live in the woods.
We are Jewish people left over.
And they followed us.
And they came.
And they saw.
Then they send right away wagons.
And they say, we were very near of liberation.
However, weeks and a month or before, we already heard loud cannons shooting.
We knew that, that the Russian are coming back.
The Germans are losing.
We heard planes flying.
We saw them, some.
He said, they're only maybe 30 kilometers from here.
We are partisans.
We cleared the way.
They took us over.

And well, the mentality was that we thought because we suffered so much, we would be treated differently, not knowing-- me, as a child, as a, as a teenager, I didn't know that the Russian probably suffered a lot, too.
But me, thinking being a Jew, going through all that hell, I'll have special treatment.
However, they took us in their camp.
And they sent the teenagers and the women on sleighs and horses back where the Russian army was already.
But they had a speech.
The commander has a speech.
And I remember, he expressed himself-- don't act like Jews.
Help the, the Russian people.
Go to the army, you know?
And I started wondering.
I figured, well, that's exactly [INAUDIBLE] as I only had 14 years.
But I understood.
And I was very disappointed.
I figured, oh, god, here we go, right back.
So we wandered the whole night.
And we came to our little town, [INAUDIBLE].
The Russian army was there.
We were taken right away into our room.
There were men with us, some, some men that, that didn't want to take with them to the partisans, my sister, other people.
And very heavy questioning-- you are spies.
You, work for Ger-- you know?
And then slowly, slowly, it loosened up.
They saw-- then one guy came in from that city who knew two people that were with us.
They lived probably not far from that city.
And he recognized me.
He said, no, they're Jews.
And then it [? was nice. ?] They gave us food.
And then, and then they put us in on wagons the next day and pushed us farther down back to a town, Dubno.
We came to, to Dubno.
And they told us, you're on your own.
Go  ahead.
Do whatever you can.
So the first thing, you ask-- where, where are some Jews left over?
So they showed us.
There's an old synagogue.
And we went to the synagogue.
We met Jews.
And we found a house with a Polish girl.
She took us in.
And, and my sister got sick.
She had typhus.
We had to take her to the hospital And she came out of it.
And we were on our own.
So now-- About what time was this?
Was this in the--
This was in March.
End of March '44, I was liberated.
I was in Dubno among Russian.
And I wanted to go back to Brody, which was 65 kilometers.

But you couldn't go there, because what the Russian army did is they went-- they surrounded it with so many divisions, continued straight up, made a pocket, and just keep bombarding them there, you know?
And then continued-- they were much further down.
So what I did-- I start speculate.
They call it you speculated.
You, you bought merchandise in one city, in Dubno.
And you traveled 45 miles to Rivne.
And you exchanged.
You sold there, let's say, sugar.
Then you brought back flour.
So you bought shoes.
And so I was a speculator.
And that's where we lived now.
And af-- after a few times, I was caught.
They put me in a, in a children's home.
I was 14 years old.
And they told me they were going to send me in deep to Russia.
Maybe they'll educate me as a Russian officer to military school.
And I agreed to everything.
Where are your parents?
They got killed.
I don't have no parents.
That helped  you a little bit so that they gave me food.
And they put me in a little jail.
And they said, the next day, we'll send.
So at night-- it was not a jail in the sense of a jail.
It was a house, you know, for people, for kids who didn't have parents.
And at night, I run away again.
And it started again the same thing.
Meantime, my sister was married and met a man, got married in Dubno.
And we, we moved to Lwow, which is Lemberg.
We moved there.
And we lived there for a while.
When you ran away at night--
I run away from the house.
No, right back to the same city.
I mean, they didn't have any records, you know?
It was very disorganized.
And I ran.
And I came back.
And I started the same thing the next day.
You had a little vodka.
You went on the road.
You, you caught a pickup truck.
And you smuggled.
And we lived in Lwow until one day, the Russian government told all the Polish citizen, if, you want to-- if you think you're Polish, then you have till-- 90 days you have to evacuate.
So me, and my sister, and my brother-in-law went-- we went to Poland.
